data.title: From Static HTML to AI-Powered Content: The History of CMS (1995–2026)
data.excerpt: Content management systems have quietly shaped the web for three decades. From hand-coded HTML files on FTP servers to headless, AI-integrated platforms — here's the full story.
data.content:
## The FTP era (1995–2000)

Before content management systems existed, there was a developer, an FTP client, and a folder of HTML files. Every page was hand-coded. Every change required a developer. Every deployment was a manual upload to a shared server.

This was unsustainable. The web was growing exponentially, and organizations needed to publish content faster than their developers could type.

## The CMS era begins (2001–2010)

Movable Type (2001), WordPress (2003), Drupal (2001), Joomla (2005). The first generation of CMS platforms separated content from presentation. Non-developers could log in, type content, and publish. The developer's role shifted from page-builder to template-builder.

WordPress won this era decisively. By 2010, it powered 15% of all websites. Its plugin ecosystem made it infinitely extensible — and infinitely complex.

## The headless revolution (2011–2020)

Mobile killed the monolith. When your CMS needs to serve a website, an iOS app, an Android app, and a smartwatch, the traditional "CMS renders HTML" model breaks. Contentful (2013), Prismic (2013), and Sanity (2017) pioneered the headless approach: the CMS is an API, the frontend is separate.

Developers regained control. But content editors lost their WYSIWYG preview. And the complexity budget simply shifted from plugins to API integrations.

## The AI moment (2023–present)

Large language models changed everything. Content generation, translation, SEO optimization, image analysis — tasks that took hours now take seconds. But the CMS platforms built for the pre-AI world aren't ready.

They treat AI as a plugin. A sidebar widget. A "generate" button on a text field. This is the WordPress plugin pattern repeating itself.

The CMS of the AI era needs AI at its core. Schema-driven content that AI can reason about. Native operations for generation, translation, and optimization. Field-level protection (AI Lock) to keep humans in control. And machine-readable output (llms.txt, MCP) so other AI agents can discover and query your content.

That's what we're building.
data.author: Christian Broberg
